content.assessment: T3 is the proposal most directly structurally analogous to Poland's 1999 reform: both raise the age of first tracking via a unified comprehensive lower-secondary phase. The lens surfaces two critical Polish lessons: (1) Poland's 1999 delay correlated with PISA gains and reduced socio-economic gaps, particularly for formerly vocational-track students, supporting the plausibility of the structural mechanism (F-live-polish_reform-03); and (2) Poland's 2017 reversal, which advanced tracking by one year without phased transition, caused severe short-term implementation disruption (unplaced students, school closures, teacher job losses), directly validating T3's own explicit phasing risk-mitigation. Notably, Poland's own 2018 PISA results attributed to strong performance actually reflected the outgoing (pre-reversal) cohort, not the new structure — a caution about attribution that cuts both ways: it strengthens the case that the comprehensive structure (gimnazjum) was responsible for earlier gains, but also means the new 8+4 system's true effects remain unverified (F-live-polish_reform-06).
opportunities: A well-phased implementation (unlike Poland's abrupt 10-month reversal) could avoid the acute disruption Poland experienced, if T3's sequencing commitment is honored in practice (F-live-polish_reform-04, F-live-polish_reform-05)
strengths: Poland's 1999 comprehensive-stage reform shows structural delay of tracking correlates with measurable PISA gains and reduced socio-economic performance gaps (F-live-polish_reform-03)

T3 explicitly designs for phased transition, directly responding to the demonstrated risk that Poland's unphased 2017 reversal caused acute disruption (3,173 unplaced students in Warsaw, ~7,000 school closures, 10,000 potential teacher job losses) (F-live-polish_reform-04, F-live-polish_reform-05)
threats: The Polish experience demonstrates that comprehensive-stage structural reforms, even with a documented multi-year track record of apparent success, remain politically reversible, meaning T3's structural gains could be undone by a future government just as Poland's were (F-live-polish_reform-04)

Implementation sequencing failure, if T3's phasing commitment is not honored, would risk reproducing Poland's disruption pattern (unplaced cohorts, closures, job losses) at Hungarian scale (F-live-polish_reform-05)
weaknesses: The durability of structural delay's benefits is questionable given how easily Poland's reform was reversed after nearly two decades, indicating structural reforms of this kind face high political reversibility risk regardless of initial evidence of success (F-live-polish_reform-04)

Attribution of Poland's positive 2018 PISA results to the old comprehensive structure rather than the new reversed structure shows that outcome evidence in structural reforms can be confounded by cohort transition effects, a methodological caution relevant to evaluating any future Hungarian reform's early results (F-live-polish_reform-06)
